attributes.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic requesting a medical certificate for her eight-year-old daughter who was unwell with gastroenteritis last week. You had seen and treated the daughter five days ago though the daughter attended with her father. The mother explains she needs the certificate backdated as her employer requires it for carer's leave. She offers to pay the full consultation fee.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate response?
options: Provide a certificate dated for today's date
Suggest the mother obtain a statutory declaration instead
Ask the father to request the certificate instead
Provide a backdated certificate as you had seen the child
Provide a backdated certificate as the mother is willing to pay
attributes.explanation:
This scenario addresses the ethical and professional obligations surrounding medical certificate provision. Medical certificates are legal documents that must reflect contemporaneous assessment of a patient's condition.

Suggesting a statutory declaration whilst explaining that you cannot provide a backdated certificate would be the most appropriate response. An employer can accept statutory declarations for taking sick leave as proof. The mother would then be encouraged to ask for a medical certificate at the time of presentation in the future.

Providing a certificate dated for today would be inappropriate as you have not assessed the child's condition today and cannot verify their past illness status.

Asking the father to request the certificate would not change the fundamental issue that backdating certificates is inappropriate, regardless of which parent makes the request.

Providing a backdated certificate, even though you had seen the child, would constitute fraudulent documentation. Though the mother may have taken off time during the sickness, legal documents can only state facts and not presumptions. The fact that you had previously treated the child does not justify backdating a certificate.

Providing a backdated certificate with payment would be unethical and potentially illegal, regardless of financial compensation.

Medical practitioners have an obligation to maintain accurate and contemporaneous medical records. Backdating certificates, even with good intentions, can compromise professional integrity and may have legal consequences. This applies equally to requests from family members, friends, or regular patients.
learningPoints: Medical certificates must only be issued following a contemporaneous medical consultation with the patient.
Backdating medical certificates or issuing them for third parties without consultation breaches professional standards.
Medical practitioners must maintain appropriate professional boundaries, even with family members.
references: Fairwork Ombudsman. Notice and medical certificates. Australian Government. 2024.  Available at: https://www.fairwork.gov.au/leave/sick-and-carers-leave/paid-sick-and-carers-leave/notice-and-medical-certificates
